data.title: Whitecap Resources: Strong Production In Q3, Softer Energy Prices
data.description:
Overview

Whitecap Resources (TSX:WCP:CA)(OTCPK:WCPRF) is a Canadian oil and natural gas producer that has graduated to a large-cap producer following its recent acquisition of Veren. That acquisition closed in May of this year.

The company has a stellar operating history, which has managed to grow its production on an absolute level and on a per-share basis over the last few years and over the long term. Whitecap has now raised its production guidance for 2025 to 305,000 boe/d and the current production volume is around 370,000 boe/d.

This is a company I have covered quarterly for a few years, and my prior articles can be found here. Whitecap has also been in my portfolio a couple of times over the last few years. The second holding period in 2025 turned out to be short-lived after the stock price went from very oversold in April to more fairly valued just a few months later.

Today's article will focus on the Q3 2025 result that was released yesterday after the close. Whitecap will host a webcast today at 11 am ET, which can be watched here.

The total return of Whitecap has been modest this year, up 4% in U.S. dollar terms. However, when we consider that the WTI price has declined by as much as 15% and many energy companies have struggled, this is a relatively decent stock price performance by Whitecap in 2025.

Q3 2025 Result

Q3 2025 was the first full quarter including the Veren assets. Last quarter was a little bit messy with the acquisition closing mid-quarter, so some quarter-over-quarter comparisons are less useful now. Whitecap reported a strong production of 374,623 boe/d in Q3, with 61% liquids. The production per share in Q3 was 4.7% higher than Q3 last year.

The most recent quarter was yet another quarter with relatively soft energy prices. Whitecap reported a total realized price of C$48.17/boe in the most recent quarter, down 6.5% compared to Q2 2025. The crude oil price was marginally higher quarter-over-quarter, while the natural gas price decreased by as much as 29.2% quarter-over-quarter, which had an outsized impact on the total realized price.

A strong production and softer energy prices led to a decent funds flow of C$897M in Q3 2025. The funds flow per share was C$0.73, which was a 4% decline compared to the prior quarter, and free funds flow per share was C$0.29, down 11% compared to Q2. The company still has the dividend well covered.

Whitecap got a larger amount of debt with the Veren acquisition and has recently issued some more senior notes at an attractive interest rate of 3.761%. The net debt was C$3.3B in the most recent quarter, up marginally compared to Q2.

If we annualize the Q3 2025 funds flow, the net debt to funds flow comes to 0.9, which is a manageable leverage ratio for this investment grade rated company, using what at least I consider to be softer energy prices.

In the table below, we can see some figures on a C$/boe basis for Q3 compared to Q3 last year. Royalties and opex are down, while transportation expenses have increased. The lower energy prices might explain some of the decreases compared to Q3 2024, but royalties on the Veren assets have been lower. Interest expenses have also decreased marginally, and the lower interest rate in the market has offset the higher debt load per production unit. Overall, the differences compared to last year are relatively small.

Conclusion

Whitecap is a high-quality oil & natural gas producer. The company has a stellar operating history, a consistent growth trajectory, and plenty of reserves.

Whether Whitecap is cheap or not probably depends on energy prices and what multiple is appropriate for a company of this size and quality. Compared to larger Canadian peers, the forward-looking EV to EBITDA margin is still somewhat lower at 4.5. The dividend yield of 7.1% is also relatively attractive for a company of its size. So, there is likely some re-rate potential here over time.

As illustrated in figure 2, Whitecap has traded marginally higher while the WTI price has declined 15% this year, which has not improved the valuation using strip prices. With that said, I think current energy prices are on the low side, and it would not be overly aggressive to consider a WTI price 15-20% higher as a mid-cycle price, which would leave plenty of upside for Whitecap.

Based on the 2026 guidance that was released yesterday, the company is projecting a free cash flow yield of 10% against the market cap and 8% against the enterprise value.

However, I would argue that there are many oil & natural gas producers that are trading with low earnings multiples and relatively high dividend yields. So, I remain neutral on Whitecap for the time being. I would need to see a 10-15% share price decline for me to be interested in adding Whitecap to the portfolio, which I think we are unlikely to see without more downside in energy prices.

data.title: Here's Why TSMC Didn't Rise Despite Record Profits In Q3 2025
data.description:
The release of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company’s (NYSE:TSM) third quarter (Q3) results for its Fiscal Year (FY) 2025 on the 16th of October 2025 prompted different market reactions in the U.S. vis-à-vis its Taiwan-listed ticker (“2330.TW”):

While the Taiwan-listed ticker soared before moderating in the present week, the U.S.-listed ticker (wherein each ADS comprises 5 ordinary shares) showed a distinctly bearish flavor. The factors behind this are directly related to the outlook on AI chip buy-ins as well as the ongoing U.S.-China trade tensions.

Trend Drilldown

Taiwanese investors weren’t entirely unjustified, as trends in the year till date indicate relative to the previous FY. As of the first nine months (“9M”) of the current FY, revenues already equal those made in FY 2024.

Meanwhile, the cost of revenue—as well as research and development (R&D) expenses—is running at a slight discount relative to the previous FY.

However, this isn’t exactly a repeat of the previous FY’s performance. If current trends continue, earnings per share are trending towards a 13-14% increase over the previous FY, while revenue would be 33-35% higher. In the previous FY, a lower growth in revenue had resulted in a higher unlocking of earnings.

This was likely one factor behind the U.S.-listed ticker that is generally considered to be a “high-conviction” name in the midst of the ongoing AI Hype enveloping major tech stocks. Without a high rate of growth in the bottom line evident in trends, some of this conviction is likely faltering among many U.S.-based investors.

Another factor is the ongoing China-U.S. trade dispute.

TSMC's China Problem

The ongoing dispute over trade between the U.S. and China is another layer on top of long-standing allegations made over the years in the U.S. legislature on a bipartisan basis over Chinese firms essentially stealing U.S.-developed technology. This has led to restrictions on both the export of advanced chips as well as advanced chipmaking technology for a number of years.

While the likes of American chipmaker Nvidia and Dutch chipmaking tool manufacturer ASML adapted to the restrictions by selling less advanced technology to Chinese clients, this year has seen a ratcheting up of pressure to restrict exports even more. Earlier in the month, the Dutch government seized control of Nexperia, a Chinese-owned chipmaker based in the Netherlands, on grounds of “serious governance shortcomings.” China-based Wingtech—Nexperia's parent company—has been on the U.S. government’s watchlist as a “national security concern” since December 2024.

The restriction of technology exports to China forms the crux of a key conundrum from TSMC. From Q4 2018 through Q3 2025, smartphones have gone down from 53% to 30% of the company’s revenue. The company stated in its earnings that advanced technologies—denoting the company’s current 3-nanometer, 5-nanometer, and 7-nanometer process nodes—typically used in AI-relevant computing and data center applications now accounted for 74% of its total revenue.

Juxtaposing this with the fact that high-performance computing is marked as accounting for 57% of revenue suggests that advanced technologies are also employed in a fair portion of smartphone-related products.

From Q4 2018 through Q3 2025, China and Asia-Pacific has stayed nearly the same at around a net 19-20% share of revenue. The current trade and technology tensions bring to the fore products from Nvidia (NVDA)—a major client of the company—and the possibility of restrictions being circumvented through re-exports. Earlier this month, U.S. and Singaporean authorities opened a probe into Megaspeed, a Singapore-headquartered cloud provider that was spun out of a China-based gaming company in 2023 and bought nearly $2 billion worth of Nvidia’s most advanced products (likely manufactured by TSMC) through its Malaysian subsidiary. Despite Nvidia stating that its inspections found nothing out of place within Megaspeed, authorities reportedly suspect that this might have been an attempt to circumvent the ban on exports to China.

The export ban brings to the fore another potential concern for the company. In an interview with Citadel Securities on the 14th of October, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang stated that U.S. technology export restrictions took Nvidia’s position in the AI infrastructure market in China from a 95% market share to 0%. Meanwhile, China-based Huawei’s Ascend 910C and 920 chips are designed and being positioned as direct competitors and substitutes for Nvidia's H100 and H20 chips, respectively. Reports suggest that the 910C uses components from TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix sourced at an earlier date and put together by Chinese companies like SMIC. Details are still cloudy on the provenance of the newer 920 chips.

If China’s new chips require components sourced from TSMC, this is a potential red flag for TSMC. While the company’s foundries producing components being used by Huawei and SMIC are likely based in Taiwan, TSMC has also received $11.6 billion in U.S. government funding for its $65 billion buildout of chipmaking facilities in Arizona. Like Nvidia, TSMC lies square in the crosshairs of both belligerents in the U.S.-China dispute.

This dispute also foretells substantial risks worldwide that would potentially affect the conviction behind the stock.

"Global" Narratives Rewritten

Over the past week, a significant amount of media attention was brought to bear on the news that TSMC's Arizona facilities have begun production of the wafers for Nvidia's Blackwell AI chips and likely promoted the idea among commentators that the global dominance of American manufacturing is within grasp. However, this may not be the case. In 2022, Morris Chang - the 92-year-old MIT "triple graduate" founder of TSMC who retired as the company's chairman in 2018 - flatly stated that America's attempt to grow its domestic chip production will be "a wasteful, expensive exercise in futility" and that the company has the data to prove it, thanks to 25 years of manufacturing experience from its plant in Oregon. Manufacturing in the US has been proven to be simply prohibitive - with multiple arrangements of American and foreign employees to staff its Oregon plant attempted but without much reduction in costs. He explained that the decision to build the Arizona facility was done at the urging of the U.S. government. However, given the high per-unit cost increase relative to production elsewhere, it will be hard for homegrown chip manufacturers to compete internationally. Currently, the company's cost of revenue and net income as a proportion of revenue are, respectively, at the lowest and highest levels seen since 2020:

By relying on subsidies, TSMC might be able to offset the cost of manufacturing in the U.S. to meet some portion of domestic demand for some time. But it would need to continue to manufacture in volume and at high-cost efficiencies elsewhere to pay off the debt it has raised to establish and operate the U.S. facilities (and which weren't covered by U.S. grants and subsidies).

One key factor of consideration among investors as to whether a company can be "high-conviction" is their potential to maintain high-growth rates. Since no single region can be expected to be in perpetual growth, this necessitates that the company have global exposure, i.e., be able to sell in different markets around the world. Trump's Season 2 in the U.S. political arena effectively upends this narrative.

While the administration's near-daily remonstrations over trade imbalances might be politically useful for Trump's base in the electorate, it conveniently denies the fact that the current state of trade historically played a large hand in easing access to goods and services at reasonable prices for that very same electorate (and other citizens). Meanwhile, U.S.-based companies had substantial allowances granted for entering markets all over the world. The administration's tariff tantrums provide a precedent for behavior by subsequent administrations: today, it's China and Russia; tomorrow, it will be India and the European Union's turn. The day after, it will be Japan and South Korea. Then, it will be Taiwan and Canada's turn. And after that might be Latin America and the ASEAN economies, and so forth.

For these other members of the global marketplace, there is an enormous incentive to shoulder the high cost of decoupling when previously there was none. Given the near centrality of semiconductors to the modern economy, a dozen TSMCs and Nvidia's, as well as a half-dozen ASMLs, could (or will) soon begin to bloom all over the world.

For the denizens of the world outside of the U.S., a diversity of choices will be the best outcome in the geopolitical sense. But for the long-term outlook for the stock of the likes of TSMC, it isn't - despite the best efforts of TSMC's C.C. Wei and Morris Chang, as well as even Nvidia's Jensen Huang. The fact remains that TSMC (and, for that matter, Nvidia) is increasingly and more inextricably linked to the fortunes of the U.S., which doesn't necessarily bode well for its position in the world at large and for the global conviction its stock enjoys.

data.title: United Rentals: Q3 Margin Pressure Points To Further Downside
data.description:
Shares of United Rentals (NYSE:URI) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining about 17%. The company has largely defied fears of a slowdown in construction activity as its shift towards specialty rentals boosts margins and cash flow. After having been a long-term fan of the company, I moved shares to a “sell” in July, as even with my admiration for management, I felt valuation was simply too expensive. Since then, shares have gained 11% while the market has added 6%, though the stock dropped 4% after-hours Thursday following disappointing results, bringing performance back in-line with the market. With updated financials, now is a good to revisit URI.

In the company’s third quarter, United Rentals earned $11.70, which missed expectations by $0.59, a shockingly large miss, even as revenue grew by 6% to $4.2 billion. Rental revenue was up 5.8%. We also saw the company trim its free cash flow guidance, primarily due to higher cap-ex. Particularly given this miss, concerns will rise about whether slowing economic activity is beginning to impact its business, especially with shares arguably priced for perfection. URI is the leading equipment rental company, but this remains a highly fragmented sector, which has enabled URI to build scale via organic and inorganic activity.

Margin pressure was larger than expected

This quarter was weighed down by larger-than-expected margin pressure, which will require ongoing observation. Adjusted EBITDA was $1.95 billion as margins came in at 46%; this was up 2% from last year. Margins were down 170 bps from 2024. URI is seeing pressure on rental gross margins as cost inflation is not fully transferring through to rental rates. Plus, its gain on the sale of used equipment is lessening. With tariffs potentially increasing equipment purchasing costs, I see scope for further margin pressure.

General rentals revenue grew by 3% to $2.4 billion, but margins were down 90 bps to 36.7%, due to higher inflation and input costs. Specialty does continue to outperform, which is why URI is focusing a disproportionate amount of capex on growing this segment. Revenue was up 11% to $1.27 billion, but even here, gross margins fell by 490 bps to 45.1%. While margins are still higher in this segment than in general rentals, it is not immune to broader input pricing pressure. As you can see, URI has significantly grown this exposure over time.

At the same time, fleet productivity improved by 2% from last year. This was a downshift from 3.2% in H1 of this year, and it is the weakest output since 2023. All of these factors point to somewhat softening demand. Additionally, used equipment sales rose by 3.7% to $333 million at a 45.9% gross margins. Margins were down by 360 bps from last year, as pricing for used equipment softened from elevated levels.

Macro activity is a concern

49% of its business is tied to industrial activity, 46% to nonresidential construction, and 5% to residential construction. Within industrial activity, increasing utility cap-ex has been a positive, and it likely will continue to be beneficial. However, with oil prices coming down, I see increasing downside risk to oil & gas spending, a headwind for URI.

It is worth noting that while nonresidential construction is significantly higher than several years ago (partly due to inflation), it has been declining since late 2023. In theory, this should be a headwind for equipment demand, all else equal. I continue to see risks skewed to further declines in nonresidential construction spending, given elevated rates and muted business confidence. Data center construction may help limit the magnitude of declines, though much of their cost is tied to chips, rather than physical construction, which is where United Rentals makes money.

Guidance was disappointing

Given these factors, I remain somewhat cautious about future demand, which makes the changes to guidance notable and surprising. As you can see below, URI largely narrowed guidance, bringing in both the low and high ends for EBITDA and operating cash flow. Interestingly, this came even as tweaked up its revenue outlook. That does imply margins being a little bit weaker than previous expectations, potentially an initial sign of some pricing pressure. URI’s free cash flow is about $300 million lower at the midpoint and is entirely lower than the prior range, given a significant ramp in cap-ex. This decision to ramp up equipment purchases even with EBITDA margins compressing and economic growth softening is aggressive.

Over time, URI has delivered incredible free cash flow for investors, aided by the fact that it can shift equipment purchase levels based on demand. It has generated $1.2 billion of free cash flow so far this year, with $3.6 billion of equipment purchases. With cap-ex rising, we are seeing free cash flow come in below prior estimates, but it still is a healthy level. I do believe spending on equipment is high given the age of the cycle.

URI can continue returning capital to investors

Importantly, URI has a solid balance sheet. United Rentals has $500 million of cash on its balance against $14.1 billion of debt. That leaves it with 1.9x net debt/EBITDA leverage, up slightly from 1.8x at the start of the year. Leverage is consistent with its 1.5-2.5x target. United Rentals also has no bond maturities until 2027, and with 35% of debt floating rate, Fed rate cuts should help to reduce interest expense.

With low leverage, URI can return cash to investors. So far this year, URI has repurchased $1.3 billion of stock. Management expects to buy back about $600 million of stock in Q4. URI primarily returns capital to investors through its buyback program, but it does also pay a 0.7% dividend yield. Its total capital return yield will be about 4% this year, and the significant rally in the share price over time has reduced the power of its buyback program.

Valuation is just too high

As you can see below, United Rentals scores poorly on Seeking Alpha’s valuation screener, getting a “D.” Shares are now trading at a premium to the sector, but more notably, we are seeing shares trading at a 40-50% premium to its own history. Now, I have argued in the past that URI had been overly cheap, given the counter-cyclical nature of its free cash flow, so some premium to its historic valuation could be merited as investors better appreciate the durability of its model. Still, we are now at a fairly extreme valuation gap to historic norms. This requires some caution in my view.

Back in July, I was targeting about $40 in free cash flow per share. Instead, it will be closer to $35. With margin pressure even stronger than feared, I continue to view its long-term target of $10 billion in EBITDA by 2028 as unlikely to be attained, given that assumes 50+% incremental margins. I view risks to demand as skewed to the downside, and it does not appear margins are bottoming. A ~4% free cash flow yield is simply not compelling for a capital intensive business with downside risk to demand in my view. I believe this earnings report is more likely to be a sign of things to come rather than a one-off, and I urge investors to exit URI with valuation still so elevated and rotate elsewhere.

data.title: Macerich Is Likely On The Path To $30 Per Share
data.description:
Some of our best investments in recent years have been mall REITs Simon Property Group (SPG) and Macerich (NYSE:MAC).

We first bought SPG following the pandemic crash at about $50 per share and later sold it for more than triple that.

MAC, on the other hand, started badly as we had first invested in the company before the pandemic crash, but we got to rectify that by doubling, tripling, and even quadrupling down following the crash, bringing our average cost basis down to just $8 per share.

Today, its share price is $18, and we have also earned significant dividend income along the way.

But what about now? Is it still worth holding for the long run?

We believe so. Here is our updated investment thesis:

Macerich: Transforming Into a Higher Quality REIT

For those of you who are not familiar with Macerich, it is the owner of the highest quality malls in the US, arguably even better than those of Simon Property Group.

The chart below is old, but it still shows you how Macerich has historically focused on malls in densely populated areas with rich local populations, significant tourism, and high barriers to entry.

Its malls also generate today the highest sales per square foot, far ahead of Simon:

Good examples from its portfolio include:

Scottsdale Fashion Square:

Los Cerritos Center:

Tysons Corner Center:

When we first invested in the company, we argued that the REIT had significant untapped potential in its mall portfolio.

Most of them are in true prime locations with no more land to be built, and MAC's properties offered significant densification and redevelopment potential.

In many cases, it could build additional residential towers on top of its malls and/or replace obsolete department stores and underutilized parking lots with new development projects.

At the same time, its malls were also rapidly transforming into mixed-use destinations with lots of services, entertainment, restaurants, grocery stores, co-workings, gyms, and other non-retail uses, which would make them more resilient and valuable than ever before.

This remains true today.

At the end of the day, real estate comes down to location, location, location... and MAC happens to own some of the best locations in the US and has significant long-term value-add potential within its current portfolio.

But the problems that MAC has historically faced are, first, its management; second, some of its weaker non-core assets; and third, its balance sheet.

The management issues were resolved when the company replaced its CEO with Jack Hsieh, who is the former CEO of Spirit Realty Capital (SRC). For context, SRC was a troubled net lease REIT, and Jack Hsieh oversaw its transformation into a higher-quality REIT and later sold the company to Realty Income (O) for a large gain. We used to own SRC as well, and during our holding period, it earned 4x higher returns than the broader REIT market:

SRC was so rewarding because its transformation into a higher-quality REIT with better assets, lower leverage, and shareholder-friendly management was eventually rewarded with a much higher valuation multiple.

Therefore, we think that Jack Hsieh is the perfect person for the job at MAC. As soon as he came in, he initiated a similar plan called the "Path Forward" to transform MAC into a higher-quality REIT that would eventually earn a higher multiple, replicating his past success at SRC.

He became the CEO effective March 1st, 2024, and his plan earned investors a near 40% return in the following year, but it has since given up most of those gains due to the growing fears of a potential near-term recession.

I would add that shortly after joining the company, he bought $2 million worth of stock in the open market, and earlier this year, he bought another $1 million following the dip.

The board was also refreshed, and the executive compensation was also changed to be heavily tied to the long-term total returns of the stock.

So the management issue is now resolved, which is the first part of MAC's transformation into a higher-quality REIT.

The next step was to improve the portfolio by refocusing on their best assets and getting rid of some of their weaker non-core assets, which were hurting the company's perception.

The new management immediately categorized all of their assets, deciding which to retain, redevelop, sell, or give back to lenders. They have already sold or given back $1.2 billion worth of assets and have $500 million more to go, which would leave them with a portfolio of 31 class A properties:

Beyond that, they have accelerated their mixed-use redevelopment projects and have now released or are currently negotiating 23 out of 26 anchor and big box replacements, most of which will reopen over the next two years. This should drive higher traffic, sales, and leasing across their malls.

The final step of the plan is the most time-consuming, and it is to improve the balance sheet. The REIT has historically been more heavily leveraged than its peers, which made it risky during the pandemic and the rising interest rate environment.

The good news is that things are rapidly improving. They have already brought their Debt-to-EBITDA down from 8.7x to 7.9x and expect to reach the low-to-mid-6s by the end of 2028.

By then, most of the new anchors will also have reopened and started contributing rental income to the REIT.

So in two years, the REIT should be done with its transformation, leaving it with a better balance sheet, the strongest mall portfolio in the US, and a shareholder-friendly management team.

They have guided that their FFO per share should reach about $2 per share in 2028 when this transformation is over.

This would price the stock at about 9x their forward stabilized 2028 FFO, and from there, they should be in a strong position to keep growing their FFO per share as they continue to capitalize on the undersupply of such high-quality malls in prime locations and pursue new development/redevelopment opportunities within their portfolio.

They should then also be in a position to hike their dividend substantially, as the deleveraging will then be complete, and their payout ratio based on 2028 FFO would be just 34%. This will also allow them to aggressively buy back shares if the stock hasn't been re-rated yet.

For context, Tanger (SKT) is today trading at 15x its current FFO, despite owning much weaker assets in my opinion and having worse long-term growth prospects. Their outlet centers generate less than 2x lower sales per square foot, are typically located in more rural locations with poor demographics, and focus a lot more on fashion and accessories, making them more susceptible to the long-term growth of e-commerce.

Assuming that MAC repriced at 15x just like SKT post-transformation, its share price would reach $30 by the end of 2028.

Today's share price is still just half of that, and this is why we think that MAC remains a compelling REIT to own for the long run.

Even if they disappoint us and don't quite reach this guidance or take another year or two to complete their transformation, the returns should still be compelling.

And knowing that Jack Hsieh is now in charge and has significant skin in the game, it is much easier for me to stay patient and let him complete this plan to unlock value. I would have been more reluctant under the previous management.

Finally, if the stock fails to rerate post-transformation, it would not surprise me if they sought to sell themselves to Simon Property Group or another major mall landlord like Brookfield.

That's what Jack Hsieh did with SRC, and it would not surprise me if he does it again.

What's the Main Risk?

The economy. Malls are somewhat cyclical, as their tenants rely on discretionary income, and many are highly leveraged. A recession would likely hurt the company's market sentiment and push its transformation back by a year or two, turning this into a longer story.

As long as you are patient and can look past the next few years, the risk-to-reward still seems very compelling at these levels.

data.title: Eli Lilly Accelerates On Obesity Data While Novo Nordisk Retreats
data.description:
Reasons To Be Bullish

With its $700+ billion market cap, Eli Lilly and Company (NYSE:LLY) is by far the world's largest healthcare company. The company's valuation has skyrocketed over the last few years because Eli Lilly excels in a rapidly growing industry of drugs to treat obesity and diabetes. Its flagship products in these areas are Mounjaro and Zepbound, which are experiencing high demand due to quite high diabetes and obesity levels across the developed world, and particularly in the U.S. And experts forecast the situation with these diseases to get worse over the long term, unfortunately.

While Mounjaro and Zepbound are LLY's fastest-growing products, the company is not only about them. There is another flagship product called Verzenio used for the treatment of breast cancer, which is another rapidly growing industry. The business mix is well-diversified as it also ensures presence in other markets, notably outpacing inflation growth, like pain treatment and neurodegenerative diseases.

As we can see from the latest quarter's revenue breakdown, the business mix is quite well diversified. And it relates not only to end markets, but to geographic diversification as well. Generating around one-third of revenues outside the country demonstrates LLY's solid international footprint and its ability to be a prominent player outside the home ground as well.

Since more than 50% of LLY's sales are generated by Mounjaro and Zepbound, let's focus on these two stars first. As we can see from the revenue breakdown, both products demonstrated aggressive YoY revenue growth. LLY's largest competitor in this business is Danish pharmaceutical giant Novo Nordisk (NVO). Eli Lilly looks ahead of its main rival in the obesity treatment business in terms of growth momentum because, during the latest earnings season, LLY's management raised their FY2025 guidance, particularly due to strong demand for their obesity care offerings, while NVO's financial outlook for 2025 became notably more conservative.

Therefore, LLY's much stronger share price performance in 2025 compared to NVO is fair. LLY lags behind the broader U.S. stock market this year because of the general weakness of the entire healthcare sector due to political uncertainty caused by some initiatives of President Trump's administration related to tariffs for the pharmaceutical industry. However, I think that Pfizer's recent deal with the government showed investors that the adverse effect from the political factors is unlikely to be significant.

There is still no deal between LLY and the government announced, but I think that there is a high probability of having one. Trump's strategic priority is to ensure that the largest companies are focusing on expanding their domestic manufacturing presence, and LLY has already taken some big steps in this direction. Among the company's official announcements of the last few weeks, I found plans to invest billions in manufacturing facilities in Texas and Virginia. The total investment in these two facilities is expected to be $11.5 billion, which is a large amount even for LLY (the company's total capex over the last ten years was below $20 billion). I think that this proactiveness and commitment to expand manufacturing capacity in the U.S. will be highly appreciated by the U.S. government. Therefore, the probability of achieving a win-win deal with the government is likely to be quite high for LLY.

With strong growth momentum, I expect LLY to continue its robust earnings surprise record. Moreover, we see that net EPS revisions over the last 90 days were positive, meaning that Wall Street analysts are also mostly optimistic. Revenue is expected to grow by 40% YoY, which will be even more impressive than Q2's 37.8% YoY growth. I am not comparing the Q3 EPS forecast of $6.06 to Q3 2024 because there were large one-off charges, which makes the YoY comparison useless.

With Q3 highly likely being a positive catalyst, there is a solid probability that LLY's valuation ratios can return to their historical averages post-earnings. As shown above, current P/E ratios are lower than the last five years' averages by double-digit percentages. The stock's low valuation quant rating is because LLY's valuation ratios are significantly higher than the sector median. However, there is an explanation for that. With its $720 billion market cap, LLY is in its own league. The second largest pharmaceutical company, Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ), market cap is around $250 billion lower, which is approximately Novo Nordisk's market cap. Among other large companies are AbbVie (ABBV), Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY), and AstraZeneca (AZN). And none of them demonstrate growth rates even close to LLY. Therefore, I think that comparing LLY's valuation ratios to peers is not a useful approach.

And the company continues investing heavily in growth, which I see from positive developments around the pipeline. For instance, Eli Lilly just scored a major win with its new pill for obesity and diabetes: Orforglipron. Until now, most of Lilly’s weight-loss drugs, like Mounjaro and Zepbound, have been injections. Orforglipron changes that by offering the same GLP-1 benefits in a tablet form. In short, it expands the whole market and could increase prescription volumes. A tablet form is much easier to distribute, store, and take, which tends to drive faster uptake and stronger brand loyalty. A pill that’s easy to use and doesn’t require refrigeration also fits more easily into people’s daily routines, supporting large-scale adoption. Another strength is that the pill isn’t just aimed at one condition. This diversity helps create a potentially durable revenue stream. From a business standpoint, the strong Phase 3 results mean a fast ROI. Because the data shows clear high efficacy and a clean safety profile, Orforgliprin has chances to move quickly through the regulatory review and into doctors’ hands. With that being said, the company can start recouping development costs sooner rather than later.

Reasons To Be Bearish

The market's sentiment around pharmaceutical companies remains cautious. We see that even after the deal with the U.S. government, Pfizer's stock did not soar even despite offering low multiples and compelling dividend yield. Therefore, even if a deal between LLY and the government is announced soon, it might not be impressive enough for investors.

The upcoming earnings release is likely to be strong, but the market's strong positive reaction is not guaranteed. This is quite a risk, especially because LLY's investors are so used to aggressive growth delivered by the company and its solid earnings surprise record. For example, LLY reported its Q2 earnings on August 7. This day, the stock crashed by 14% despite LLY delivering a wide EPS beat of $0.72. Revenue surprise was also solid, as actual sales were almost $900 million higher than estimates. Therefore, even a solid Q3 earnings release does not guarantee a rally.

Reaction to earnings might be weak also because of the current weak sentiment demonstrated by the broader U.S. equity market. After several months of excitement, the sentiment is currently in the "Fear" mode. If investors start switching to a more defensive stance for longer, growth stocks like LLY might suffer. Despite being a pharmaceutical giant, LLY is a pure growth stock as it offers investors not only high multiples compared to the sector median but also an almost invisible 0.75% forward dividend yield.

The Verdict

LLY is a Strong Buy due to its aggressive revenue growth momentum, which is accelerating. Buying before earnings is always a risk, but to me, a potential post-earnings sell-off will mean that I will be buying even more.

data.title: Navigating Volatile Markets: How To Think About Buffer ETFs
data.description:
There’s FOMO—and then there’s fear of losing.

For many investors, the fear of missing out on market gains is usually second to the pain that comes from taking a loss in their portfolios. This is why many struggle to stay invested during rocky markets. Unfortunately, this tendency can lead to knee-jerk reactions like selling during a downturn—which means skipping out on the inevitable recovery.

Enter buffer exchange-traded funds (ETFs). They aim to keep people invested by protecting investors from a certain percentage of market losses—the first 15%, in many cases. But there’s a trade-off: market gains are limited—usually to 10% or less.

This structure allows buffer ETFs to provide more predictable returns over time. The result is a smoother investment experience that can help investors stay the course during turbulent markets.

But is the sacrifice in returns worth it? Or is there another way for investors to minimize their losses while still getting meaningful returns? What about simply selling some stocks and moving to cash?

We’ll dive in and take a look shortly. But first, let’s go under the hood and learn the mechanics of buffer ETFs.

Creating the cushion

Buffer ETFs use a combination of options, or financial contracts, to limit market gains and losses over a period of time—typically 12 months. This is done in three key steps.

Step 1: Get Market Exposure

Buffer ETFs need exposure to the broader market. Most get this by purchasing a high-value “call” option to mirror the returns of a broad index like the S&P 500. Some funds may instead use futures or buy the index ETF directly, though that’s less common.

Examples:

  • Buying a call option on SPY (the S&P 500 ETF) at a $600 strike price, covering 100 shares. This provides exposure similar to owning $60,000 of SPY.
  • Buying 100 shares of SPY at $600 each, creating a $60,000 portfolio.

Step 2: Add Protection

A “put” option is bought to limit losses if the market falls. Because this is expensive, additional trades are used to offset the cost.

Example: Buying a put option for $3,400, which covers 100 shares of SPY.

Step 3: Set the Buffer and Limit Gains

To cover the cost of the protective put, two options are sold.

  1. Lower-strike put: Protects against losses up to a certain level in a “buffer zone.” Losses that go beyond this zone aren’t protected.
  2. Call option: Limits how much can be earned if the market rises above a certain level.

Together, these two options reduce or eliminate the cost of protection while defining the ETF’s range of returns.

Buffer Blueprint

Buffer ETFs use options to limit downside losses while capping market gains.

Downside protection and upside cap in a typical 1-year buffer ETF

Example: A 15% downside protection means selling a put option at a price that is 15% below SPY’s current level of $600. In this case, that’s $510. At $14 per share, that earns $1,400 total.

If SPY falls below $510, the protected put bought earlier and the sold call offset one another, leaving just exposure to SPY.

To cover the remaining cost of $2,000, a call option is sold at $660—earning $20 per share, or $2,000 total. This caps any gains if SPY rises more than 10% above the initial level of $600.

Combining steps 2 and 3 results in a zero-cost buffer that shields the portfolio from the first 15% of losses. However, if SPY falls below $510, the buffer no longer provides protection. On the upside, the portfolio participates fully in the first 10% of gains. Beyond that, the gains are limited because of the sold call option overlay, capping returns beyond a 10% increase in SPY.

At the end of the one-year period in this example, the buffer ETF resets. It starts a new set of options contracts with the same percentage buffer level and term length—but with a new upside cap, depending on current market prices.

Design School

The example above uses a one-year protection period, 15% downside protection and a 10% upside cap. In reality, these parameters can vary significantly from one buffer ETF to another. The length of the protection period, the size of the buffer and the upside cap all depend on how the fund is built.

Fund providers design buffer ETFs by adjusting three main variables:

  • How often they reset

Most buffer ETFs reset annually, but some reset every month or quarter, giving investors more flexibility and chances to re-enter the market.

  • Amount of protection

Common buffer levels range from 5% to 30%, depending on the fund. Higher protection typically means a lower cap on gains, as more money goes toward covering potential losses.

  • Fund structure

Some funds use set protection periods and reset on a specific date. Others offer continuous exposure, allowing investors to enter and exit at any time with a rolling options structure.

Price of Protection

While buffer ETFs offer a more controlled risk-return experience, it’s important to understand their drawbacks. Key among them is that investors may still see negative returns during the protection period—although these are typically smaller than what the broader market experiences. In addition, losses can be realized at the end of the protection period if the index falls below the buffer zone.

Most notably, because upside gains are capped, buffer ETFs often don’t keep up with the broader market. On average, they underperform by about 4% annually (below chart) due to this limitation, as well as the cost of downside protection. There is also a strong relationship between the size of the downside “buffer zone” and the degree of underperformance. The more protection offered against market losses, the greater the trade-off in returns.1

Buffer Underperformance

Buffer ETFs often lag the S&P 500 due to caps on upside gains and the cost of downside protection

Performance of select buffer ETFs vs. the S&P 500

Source: Bloomberg data, Jan. 2021-May 2025

Buffer or Derisk?

With these limitations in mind, are there other, more robust ways to protect your portfolio against market drops?

To explore this, let’s compare a one-year buffer strategy on 100 shares of SPY with a straightforward de-risking approach. This will show whether the complexity of buffer ETFs truly delivers better protection for defensive-minded investors—or if selling some stocks and moving to cash might be the smarter move.

Before we dive into the results, it’s important to understand that a buffer strategy is still partially sensitive to the stock market. In options terminology, this sensitivity is called “delta,” which measures how much a position's value changes in response when the price of SPY moves.

In the example we showed earlier, the buffer strategy has a delta of about 0.39. This means for every 1% move in SPY, the portfolio of options plus SPY is expected to move by about 0.39%. By comparison, a fully invested SPY position has a delta of 1.0, meaning it moves in step with the market.

Now, let’s consider an alternative approach: de-risking by moving part of the portfolio to cash. A portfolio with 39% in SPY and 61% in cash also has a delta of 0.39. This portfolio consists of 39 shares of SPY for $44,339—and $36,661 in cash.

The payoff after one year under different SPY outcomes for the buffer strategy and the de-risking strategy of 39% SPY and 61% cash2 is shown below.

Buffer Comparison

How a buffer ETF compares to a simple de-risking strategy

Performance of a buffer strategy versus a de-risking strategy

Source: Russell Investments

Depending on how SPY moves over the year, either the buffer strategy or the de-risking strategy may come out ahead. Specifically:

  • If the market moves above the upside cap or below the 15% buffer, the de-risking strategy generally performs better because the protection and gain limits no longer apply.
  • If SPY ends the year flat at $600, the buffer strategy underperforms since the cash portion of the de-risking strategy earns a little interest.
  • If SPY finishes the one-year period somewhere between a 15% loss and a 10% gain, the buffer ETF may outperform the de-risking strategy.

The key takeaway: buffer ETFs only perform better when market losses are less than 15% and gains under 10%. In a sharp selloff, a simple de-risking strategy of moving some money from stocks to cash usually outperforms. Conversely, in a strong rally, staying fully invested in stocks usually delivers higher returns.

Food for thought

Buffer ETFs can smooth out moderate market swings, but the cost of protection and their restrictions on upside gains often hold back returns. Depending on risk tolerance, investors may prefer either a straightforward de-risking strategy or the built-in protection of a buffer ETF.

1 S&P 500 performance versus three annual buffer ETFs from Jan2021 – Apr2025. US Moderate Buffer: 10% buffer (FT Vest US Equity Moderate Buffer), Conservative Buffer: 15% Buffer (Innovator S&P500 Power ETF), US Deep Buffer: 5% to 30% Buffer (FT Vest US Equity Deep Buffer). We observe a direct relationship between the size of downside buffer and the extent of underperformance.

2 We assume a cash return of 4%.

Important information pertaining to the hypothetical example: Past performance does not predict future returns. Return level is proportionately scaled in line with cash level to be overlaid. Source: Russell Investments. Assumptions: Average cash level 1.0%, 10-year history from 12/31/2023, gross of fees. Opportunity cost from not securitizing cash varies by asset allocation and time period, and is represented by horizontal bars as marked within the chart legend. Target asset allocation used: 0% cash, 74% MSCI World, 26% Global Aggregate (GBP Hedged). For illustrative purposes only. Does not represent any actual investment. Indexes are unmanaged and cannot be invested in directly. Performance benefit (net) of overlaying cash by last 5 individual calendar year is as follows: 2023:20 bps, 2022:-17bps, 2021:16bps, 2020:14bps, 2019:23bps.

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The information, analysis, and opinions expressed herein are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual or entity.

This material is not an offer, solicitation or recommendation to purchase any security.

Forecasting represents predictions of market prices and/or volume patterns utilizing varying analytical data. It is not representative of a projection of the stock market, or of any specific investment.

The general information contained in this publication should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax and investment advice from a licensed professional.

Diversification and strategic asset allocation do not assure a profit or guarantee against loss in declining markets.

Please remember that all investments carry some level of risk, including the potential loss of principal invested. They do not typically grow at an even rate of return and may experience negative growth. As with any type of portfolio structuring, attempting to reduce risk and increase return could, at certain times, unintentionally reduce returns.
